## The Wheel of Time

Imagine that you are the sun. You need a chariot to cross the sky. You need twenty-four wheels, seven horses, and walls covered in every expression of human longing. You need Konark.

The Sun Temple at Konark was built in the 13th century by King Narasimhadeva I of the Eastern Ganga Dynasty. It was designed as a colossal stone chariot — the *ratha* of Surya, the sun god — with twelve pairs of intricately carved wheels representing the months of the year, drawn by seven stone horses representing the days of the week.

## What the Stones Carry

The temple's exterior is an encyclopedia of medieval Indian life carved in chlorite and laterite stone. There are celestial nymphs (*apsaras*) frozen in mid-dance. There are war elephants, musicians, and astronomers. There are erotic panels in the Khajuraho tradition — not as shock, but as theology: Kama, the god of desire, is considered one of the four Purusharthas, the legitimate goals of human life.

The wheels are the most famous element. Each is carved with spokes that function as sundials — the shadow of each spoke tells the time of day with precision that shames most modern instruments. The artisans who built Konark were not merely architects. They were scientists.

## The Missing Spire

The main *shikhara* — the tower above the sanctum — collapsed sometime in the 18th or 19th century. No one recorded exactly when or why. The stone that remains is the *jagamohana*, the audience hall, which the British archaeologists filled with sand to stabilize it when they feared its imminent collapse.

There is something instructive in this: even in its damaged state, Konark is one of the most complex religious structures ever built. What it was when complete is almost beyond imagining.

## The Sound of the Sea

The temple was built within sight of the sea. The Bay of Bengal is visible from the upper terraces, and in ancient times, the *Padma Stone* — a powerful magnet lodged in the pinnacle — was said to keep ships from approaching the coast. Whether this is myth or physics remains unresolved.

What is certain is that the light here behaves differently. In the hour before sunset, the stone glows like heated copper. The wheels cast long shadows that slowly rotate, as they have been doing for eight hundred years, counting time in a language older than any alphabet.
